https://qbitjwt.com/https://youtu.be/I3myZagbuOE
As quantum computing continues to advance rapidly, the field of cybersecurity is facing major upheavals.
Existing cryptographic algorithms, like RSA and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) — the technologies currently securing almost all web communications — are under threat of future quantum decryption attacks.
To ensure the safety of sensitive information from “harvest now, decrypt later” attack vectors, industry players are migrating to Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC).
JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) form the core of contemporary API authentication and single sign-on (SSO) solutions.
If you want to upgrade your authorization layer, you need to learn how to generate quantum-safe tokens.
Luckily, you do not need a degree in theoretical physics to get started with quantum cryptography development.
With QBIT JWT, an advanced developer toolkit that enables effortless encoding and decoding of post-quantum and hybrid JWTs, this mission is no longer impossible.
Here’s a comprehensive, step-by-step guide on how to leverage QBIT JWT for building future-proof applications.
What is a Post-Quantum JWT?
Regular JWTs are using classical signature algorithms (for instance, RS256 or ES256) for ensuring data integrity.
Post-Quantum JWT employs modern, lattice-based or hash-based digital signature algorithms like:

• ML-DSA (Module-Lattice-based Digital Signature Algorithm)

• Falcon

• SPHINCS+
which are standardizations from NIST that guarantee robust security against classical and quantum-level cyberattacks.
Moreover, in order to smooth the migration phase, security experts are using hybrid signatures (e.g., RS256+ML-DSA-65).
In such a case, a regular algorithm and quantum-resistant algorithm are used together, making sure that even if one of the mathematical foundations behind it gets broken, the token will be safe.
How to Decode and Verify Post-Quantum JWTs
Once you’ve received a post-quantum token or want to check the structure of a quantum-resistant JWT, you can do it with ease using the JWT Decoder tab on QBIT JWT.
Step 1: Input Your JWT
Visit the JWT Decoder tab on qbitjwt.com.
Input your JWT into the JWT input field on the left.
Step 2: Review the Decoded Header & Payload
The platform will parse the JWT and split it into two important JSON objects on the right-hand side of the interface.
Decoded Header
As you look into the "alg" parameter, instead of "RS256" you will see "ML-DSA-65" or other post-quantum/hybrid algorithms.
Decoded Payload
It contains common JWT claims like:

• "sub" (subject)

• "iss" (issuer)

• "exp" (expiration time)
and any custom claims like "role".
Step 3: Perform Signature Verification
Scroll down to the Verification Setup section, where QBIT JWT automatically recognizes the needed verification algorithm.
In order to validate the cryptographic signature:

• Input the respective Secret / Public Key (if it’s needed, QBIT JWT will automatically generate a demonstration key for you).

• Click Verify Signature.

• Review the Signature Verified panel.
Once the validation is complete, you’ll see a "status": "verified" block, along with the evaluation of the token validity constraints.
How to Encode a Post-Quantum JWT
Should you develop or test an application and need to create a quantum-resistant token, move on to the JWT Encoder tab.
Step 1: Define Token Claims
Input your desired token claims in the Payload JSON text area.
Customize the fields that you want to include in your token, like user IDs, scopes, or session duration.
Step 2: Choose a Post-Quantum or Hybrid Algorithm
Use the Encode algorithm dropdown menu.
There are plenty of post-quantum cryptographic algorithms available in QBIT JWT.
Classical Algorithms

• RS256

• HS256

• ES256
Post-Quantum (PQC)

• ML-DSA-44

• ML-DSA-65

• ML-DSA-87

• Falcon-512

• SPHINCS+-SHA2-128f
and many others.
Hybrid Algorithms
Combinations of classical and post-quantum algorithms, e.g.:

• RS256+ML-DSA-65
Step 3: Generate the JWT

• Input your private key/signing secret into the Signing Secret / Private Key box.

• Click Encode JWT.
Your brand new token will appear in the left-hand field.
At the same time, you will be able to see the live decoded output and validated status of your freshly created post-quantum credential.
Why Developers Should Care about Post-Quantum Migration Now
Migrating to post-quantum infrastructure is not something you should do in a few years.
Major tech infrastructure players and privacy-focused platforms are already implementing PQC technologies.
Using developer-friendly tools like QBIT JWT will allow you to:

• Test token payloads.

• Measure changes in performance overheads (because post-quantum keys and signatures are larger than classical ones).

• Make your migration journey as smooth as possible.
Post-quantum authentication preparation today helps developers build systems ready for the next generation of cybersecurity challenges.
